channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: A Startup Is Trying to Buy PayPal… Craziest Deal of 2026! | E2312
gist: On a This Week in Startups VC roundtable (host Alex with Eric Bahn of Hustle Fund and Jeff Morris Jr. of Chapter One), the panel dissects Stripe's reported $50B+ bid to take PayPal private and what it signals about public-vs-private market dynamics, then ranges across AI coding tools reaching 'AGI' levels, why startups avoid building custom model evals until forced to, why Chapter One is betting on LA/physical-AI deals to dodge fast-followers, the AI consumer-startup drought outside health hardware, AI regulation politics, crypto's pivot to stablecoins/RWAs over DAOs, and a Lloyd's-of-London-style space insurance thesis.

insights.claim: Stripe, a private company, is reportedly trying to acquire PayPal, a public company, for more than $50 billion — an unusual direction for M&A that highlights how large private companies can now out-muscle public incumbents.
insights.speaker: Alex, host
insights.so_what: Watch for more well-capitalized private companies acquiring struggling public incumbents rather than the reverse; PayPal's minority shareholders face a steep markdown if the deal closes.

insights.claim: PayPal's share price has fallen from a peak above $300 around 2021 to a reported take-private offer near $16.50/share, illustrating how quickly a dot-com-era brand can lose the bulk of its market value.
insights.speaker: Alex, host
insights.so_what: A ~95% peak-to-trough decline in a former blue-chip fintech is a reminder that brand recognition alone doesn't protect valuation once growth and culture stall.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn argues large tech companies follow a predictable arc: they start scrappy and innovative, then professionalize as career-focused hires join and the culture shifts toward internal promotion politics rather than vision — a pattern he sees playing out at PayPal.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Investors can use this maturity-arc framework to flag aging tech incumbents at risk of stagnation or takeover before the market fully prices it in.

insights.claim: PayPal's newly installed CEO (appointed around February 2026) told investors on the earnings call there are areas where the company can make large cuts, which Jeff Morris Jr. reads as confirmation of internal bloat and cultural calcification.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Signals that a cost-cutting/restructuring narrative at PayPal predates and likely factors into the Stripe acquisition talks.
insights.quote: places where we can make large cuts in in the company

insights.claim: Because it is private, Stripe can pursue a large, uncertain acquisition like PayPal without near-term public-market scrutiny of margins or EPS impact, giving it a longer time horizon than a public acquirer would have.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Private-company balance-sheet flexibility is becoming a real competitive advantage in large-scale M&A, not just financing structure.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn can name fewer than 10 Silicon Valley tech companies (citing Microsoft and Oracle as examples) that have avoided the multi-decade ossification-and-decline trajectory seen at Yahoo and now PayPal.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Durable multi-decade tech compounders are rare; investors should treat sustained reinvention as the exception, not the base case, when underwriting long-duration holds.

insights.claim: Webflow underwent major layoffs and a re-architecture around May 2026 to escape the same kind of cultural bloat that afflicted PayPal, after early investor Eric Bahn says the company's Web 2.0-era browser-design product got 'rug pulled' by the AI-native design paradigm.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A former category leader (Webflow) actively cutting staff to regain a 'start-uppy' pace is a live case study in whether cost discipline alone can restore competitiveness against AI-native challengers.

insights.claim: Fintech startup Flex closed a $70 million Series B1 at a $1.2 billion valuation around the same time as bank earnings came in strong, prompting Eric Bahn to float — as an untested hypothesis — that incumbent banks may start acquiring mid-tier, overlooked fintech startups.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A wave of bank-led fintech roll-up M&A, if it materializes, would be a departure from the current AI-dominated deal narrative and worth tracking via bank earnings commentary.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. argues the line between public and private companies is dissolving because private stock (e.g., Stripe shares via platforms like Solium) now trades with real liquidity, and companies like Anthropic and OpenAI issue informal quarterly performance updates that move public-market sentiment.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: If private companies effectively offer public-like liquidity and disclosure, the traditional rationale for going public (access to capital) weakens further, delaying IPO supply.

insights.claim: A recent dispute between USV and Andreessen Horowitz over Series H share terms — with conflicting claims about what was agreed — is cited as evidence that private markets lack the data transparency and price discovery of public markets.
insights.speaker: Alex, host
insights.so_what: Institutional-grade disputes over basic deal terms in late-stage private rounds signal governance and documentation risk that public-market investors don't face.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. expects more companies to go public over the next one to two years, pointing to SpaceX's IPO and anticipated Anthropic/OpenAI activity, but cautions that going public is no guarantee of upside since SpaceX is already trading below its IPO price.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: A rebound in IPO volume is likely near-term, but investors should expect a wide dispersion of post-IPO performance rather than assuming a rising tide.

insights.claim: Controlling who holds secondary shares has become a major operational burden for late-stage private CEOs, especially government contractors like Anduril, which must screen out foreign or unwanted investors (e.g., shell entities tied to sanctioned jurisdictions) to avoid jeopardizing government contracts.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Defense/critical-industry startups increasingly need active cap-table governance infrastructure, not just fundraising — a service/tooling gap worth watching.
insights.quote: we're trying not to. We're staying private and we're exercising pretty strict control over their their equity.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. predicts tight, Anduril-style control over secondary-share trading will become the industry norm as more stories emerge of SPV managers disappearing with investor funds, likely prompting SEC-level intervention to clean up the secondary market.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Expect regulatory scrutiny of SPV structures and secondary-market intermediaries to increase, which could raise compliance costs for retail-facing secondary platforms.

themes.theme: AI Coding Tools Hitting an 'AGI' Ceiling

insights.claim: Hustle Fund runs with just 7 full-time staff (22 total including contractors) and only 4 investors managing roughly 700 portfolio companies, leaning heavily on internally built AI software for deal sourcing, outreach automation, and preliminary deal assessment.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A 700-company portfolio run by 4 investors is a concrete data point on how far AI tooling can compress venture-firm headcount needs — a template smaller emerging managers may copy.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn believes current-generation coding AI tools (referencing Fable-based tooling and Opus) now give code-quality feedback at the level of a senior engineer, and calls this 'AGI' having effectively arrived within the coding domain specifically.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: If coding assistance has reached senior-engineer-level judgment, expect further compression of engineering headcount needs at startups and continued margin expansion for software companies.
insights.quote: this is AGI. I think it's actually happened within coding

insights.claim: Eric Bahn claims most venture capitalists have never actually used coding-AI tools like Claude Code in a real, hands-on way despite publicly urging founders to become AI-native — calling the industry's own AI adoption 'ass-backwards.'
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A gap between VCs' public AI advocacy and private AI usage suggests founders should discount generic 'be AI-native' advice from investors who haven't tested the tools themselves.
insights.quote: the majority of VCs have not touched cloud code once

insights.claim: Chapter One won an allocation in Better Up (invested in 2025) by building a working web app with user authentication using the founder's own infrastructure and demoing it at the second meeting — a task Jeff Morris Jr. says now takes roughly 5-10 minutes with current coding-AI tools.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Demonstrating hands-on product-building capability during diligence is becoming a differentiated way for investors to win competitive deal allocations.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. predicts frontier AI labs' next major research push is 'physical AI' (robotics), interpreting labs' pivot toward robotics teams (e.g., OpenAI's) as an implicit signal that coding and other digital-native tasks are viewed as close to solved.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Capital and talent flows into robotics/physical-AI teams at major labs are a leading indicator worth tracking for the next hardware-software investment wave.
insights.quote: we conquered coding, and now we're going to um you know, focus on the physical world

themes.theme: Model Lock-in, Custom Evals, and Competitive Moats

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. estimates only a handful of pre-Series A companies in his portfolio build their own model evals in-house; most startups instead chase whichever model is cheapest until they reach product-market fit, at which point building defensibility against OpenAI and Anthropic becomes urgent.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Custom-eval tooling for post-PMF, pre-Series-A startups looks like an underserved market gap that Jeff Morris Jr. explicitly says needs to be built.

insights.claim: The real data-leakage risk to startups using major AI labs' models isn't training data (which labs contractually disclaim using) but usage metadata — tool-call frequency and patterns that Satya Nadella has said can itself be informative to the model provider.
insights.speaker: Alex, host
insights.so_what: Startups building on frontier-model APIs should think about metadata exposure (call patterns, customer segmentation signals) as a competitive risk, not just raw data/prompt content.

insights.claim: At the pre-seed stage, Hustle Fund's ~700 portfolio companies will use whatever AI model is cheapest and available, with open-source models gaining traction purely on cost grounds; eval-building only becomes a real conversation once a company has a proven business post-PMF.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Model-agnosticism at pre-seed means vendor-lock-in and eval-moat strategies aren't relevant filtering criteria for the earliest-stage investors yet.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. uses an F1 racing analogy: frontier models today are as close to each other in capability as F1 constructors' cars, so the real competitive edge for startups comes from company-specific fine-tuning and evals ('pit-stop strategy'), though he says most startups he sees are still at 'entry-level Miata spec.'
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Investors should stop over-weighting which frontier model a startup uses and instead evaluate whether the team has built proprietary tuning/eval infrastructure around it.
insights.quote: what the teams don't want to share is like their pit stop strategies for the next race, like the racing line they're going to take, like the tire management

themes.theme: Chapter One's LA / Physical-AI Strategy to Avoid Fast Followers

insights.claim: Chapter One made 8 investments in the past year, 6 of them in Los Angeles and only 1 in Silicon Valley, deliberately avoiding the Bay Area software cluster where competitors can fast-follow a proven idea almost immediately.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Geographic and sector deconcentration away from Silicon Valley software is an explicit sourcing edge Chapter One is underwriting capital against.

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. observes that physical-AI/hardware startup clusters form more slowly than software clusters because they require combined hardware-and-software execution and often sit inside regulated, legacy industries — a structural moat he views as an investment advantage.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Regulatory and hardware complexity that slows competitor formation is being used explicitly as a moat-selection filter by at least one active venture fund.
insights.quote: I find the clusters are slower to form within physical AI than software, which to me is an advantage

insights.claim: Chapter One's LA-area bets include Airbase (a new bank that obtained the fastest US banking license on record, backed by Palmer Luckey), General Intuition (pivoted from gaming-clip app Medal TV, using that data plus ex-DeepMind UK talent to build physical-world/defense-drone AI), and Afero (an ex-Tesla team building software for auto manufacturers) — each chosen for unique data, regulatory positioning, or team rather than pure software speed.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: A concrete checklist (proprietary data + regulatory moat + non-Silicon-Valley team) for sourcing defensible AI-adjacent startups outside the crowded software fast-follower zone.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn believes capital flooding into narrow, newly discovered AI-agent infrastructure niches (citing Oak, a company building a shared identity layer for agents that raised $60 million) is partly explained by mega venture funds needing to deploy large AUM into trendy categories, independent of proven total addressable market.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Rapid multi-company funding sprees in narrow AI-infra niches (like agent identity) may be more AUM-deployment-driven than demand-driven, a caution flag for late entrants and follow-on investors.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn's investing mantra at Hustle Fund is that 'great hustlers look like anyone and come from anywhere,' and he argues that prolonged immersion in the Bay Area's insular tech culture erodes founders' and investors' originality of thought.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A geography-agnostic sourcing thesis (backed by Hustle Fund's actual portfolio) that investors overweighting Bay-Area-based founders may be missing comparable opportunity elsewhere.
insights.quote: great hustlers look like anyone and come from anywhere

themes.theme: Consumer AI Drought: Health Wins, Social Still Extraction-Driven

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. says the biggest surprise of the AI wave so far is how few consumer companies have used AI in genuinely novel ways; early attempts to inject generative content into Instagram-style feeds largely flopped because users still want to connect with real people and real content.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Consumer AI-content-feed startups betting on synthetic content substitution for social connection are working against a demonstrated user preference for authenticity.
insights.quote: that to me was a sign that people actually do want to connect with real people and real content

insights.claim: Consumer hardware/health startups such as Whoop and Oura have been the standout, under-recognized winners of the current consumer-startup era, while consumer social and marketplace startups have lagged behind — a gap Jeff Morris Jr. attributes to health being a large, under-served need incumbents ignored.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Consumer health/wearables remains a comparatively underappreciated category for new capital deployment relative to social/marketplace consumer bets.
insights.quote: the wave of consumer hardware startups that have actually succeeded over the past several years is up is very understated

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. is bullish on 'AI-native fintech' products that give an AI agent its own bank account to act as an autonomous financial manager, predicting a breakout company will emerge in this category — but doubts it will be Robinhood, whose product surface he sees as already too crowded/busy.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Agent-managed personal-finance products are a specific, actionable category to watch for new entrants rather than assuming incumbents like Robinhood default-win it.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn's fund backed Sage Haven, a safety-focused messaging app functioning as a guarded parallel to iMessage that lets parents and trusted community members message children, reflecting his broader conviction that consumer AI/safety products for families remain underbuilt.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Child-safety-focused messaging/communication infrastructure is a specific consumer sub-category with active early-stage investment interest.

insights.claim: Both guests note that X's algorithm change (implemented the day of recording) sharply improved feed enjoyment, which Eric Bahn interprets as evidence that the 2005-2015-era 'joyful' style of social networking was displaced post-2015 by extraction-oriented, engagement-maximizing feed design — leaving room for a new startup to rebuild that experience without the extraction.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: A platform-level algorithm tweak visibly moving user sentiment suggests real appetite (and white space) for a chronological/relationship-first social product distinct from engagement-optimized incumbents.

themes.theme: AI Regulation Politics, Youth Social-Media Limits, and Crypto's Institutional Pivot

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. argues Anthropic's (Dario Amodei's) early public messaging around AI-driven job loss was strategically damaging, undermining the public and political support the AI industry now needs to justify large infrastructure build-outs.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Lab communications strategy around job-displacement messaging is being blamed by an industry insider for eroding the political capital needed for data-center/infrastructure permitting and support.
insights.quote: I was actually pretty shocked that um Dario and Anthropic, like a lot of their early comms, to me, even being in the in in the industry, were really scary

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. is broadly anti-regulation on which AI models can be released, arguing government oversight committees lack technical proximity to the technology and change unpredictably across administrations, and notes that Anthropic and OpenAI now maintain large Washington D.C. policy teams to manage this directly with both parties.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Regardless of which party controls Washington, expect continued heavy direct lab-to-government lobbying on model release policy rather than a stable, depoliticized regulatory framework.

insights.claim: Eric Bahn supports raising the effective minimum age for unrestricted social media access to 16-18 (citing UK/European precedent), comparing unrestricted youth social-media use to giving children cigarettes given that brain development, especially in males, continues to roughly age 25-26.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: Growing VC-class sentiment favoring youth social-media age restrictions could presage more startup/product opportunity in age-verification and safe-messaging tooling (paralleling his Sage Haven investment).
insights.quote: brains are developing especially for men until we we don't stop developing until we're like 25 or 26

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. says stablecoins have dominated most large fintechs' product roadmaps for the past three years and will continue to, while the more novel current trend is bringing real-world assets (RWA) like equities on-chain to enable 24/7 trading and borrowing/lending — a direction Robinhood is already pursuing.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Stablecoin infrastructure and on-chain RWA tokenization remain the highest-conviction, most durable crypto investment theses versus more speculative categories.
insights.quote: stablecoins have been i it for the past 3 years, a huge part of the of the focus

insights.claim: Jeff Morris Jr. says DAOs (decentralized autonomous organizations) have effectively died as a venture-backable governance model — a reversal from an era when VCs pitched entire 200-member DAOs for funding-round allocation — with venture and crypto both consolidating around founder-controlled 'founder mode' structures instead.
insights.speaker: Jeff Morris Jr., Chapter One
insights.so_what: Governance-token/DAO-based fundraising structures are no longer a credible venture category; capital and talent have moved to founder-controlled institutional crypto infrastructure instead.
insights.quote: you'd have like 200 members of the DAO, and the VCs would have to pitch the entire DAO on why they should take the VCs' money for the next funding round

themes.theme: Space Insurance as an Emerging Asset Class

insights.claim: Eric Bahn frames Hustle Fund's portfolio company Charter Space, a launch-and-in-orbit insurance underwriter, as a modern Lloyd's of London — a company built on the thesis that rising launch volume and orbital payload value (satellites, habitats, potential data centers) will require growing risk-transfer infrastructure, similar to how 17th-century maritime insurance emerged for shipping trade.
insights.speaker: Eric Bahn, Hustle Fund
insights.so_what: As launch cadence and orbital asset value rise, dedicated space-insurance underwriting is a nascent but potentially large new insurance sub-category to track.
insights.quote: I like to think of this company as Lloyd's of London
